exercises.title: Write a Reusable Scalar Function in Plain SQL
concepts: calling a function in a query
exercises.problemHtml:
A rule repeated in twelve queries becomes twelve places to fix when the rule changes. A function moves that rule into the database once, where every query and every application language sees the same definition. The simplest kind needs no procedural language at all: LANGUAGE sql takes a single expression, and PostgreSQL can inline it into the calling query, so the abstraction is usually free.

Create a function that applies a percentage discount to a price:

  • discounted(price NUMERIC, pct NUMERIC) RETURNS NUMERIC, returning price reduced by pct percent, rounded to two decimals.
  • Declare it IMMUTABLE — the same inputs always give the same result, which lets the planner fold it into constants and use it in an index expression.
  • Mark it STRICT so a NULL input yields NULL instead of running the body.
  • Call it over the products table: return name, price and discounted(price, 10) for every product, ordered by name.
  • Also call it once with a NULL price to show the STRICT behaviour.

Use CREATE OR REPLACE so re-running your script is harmless.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table: Cable 5.00, Keyboard 50.00, Monitor 150.00, Mouse 20.00.
exercises.outputSpec: Four rows ordered by name with a 10 percent discount applied — Cable 4.50, Keyboard 45.00, Monitor 135.00, Mouse 18.00 — and a NULL result for the NULL input.
exercises.constraints: LANGUAGE sql with a single expression body. Declare IMMUTABLE and STRICT. Do not write the discount arithmetic at the call sites.

examplesJson.explanation: 50 minus 10 percent, rounded to two decimals.
Rounding to two decimals keeps the result a valid money value.
STRICT means the body is never entered when any argument is NULL.

hintsJson: A LANGUAGE sql function body is just a SELECT returning one value.
ROUND(numeric, 2) keeps money values sane.
IMMUTABLE tells the planner the result depends only on the arguments.
STRICT is shorthand for RETURNS NULL ON NULL INPUT.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Three declarations carry the weight here, and each one tells the planner something it can act on. LANGUAGE sql means the body is a single query rather than a procedural block, so PostgreSQL can inline it into the caller — the generated plan looks as if you had written the arithmetic by hand, and the abstraction costs nothing at runtime. A plpgsql equivalent would be a black box the planner must call once per row.

IMMUTABLE promises the result depends only on the arguments: no table reads, no now(), no session settings. That promise lets the planner evaluate the call once for constant arguments and, more importantly, allows the function in an expression index such as CREATE INDEX ON products (discounted(price, 10)). The promise is not checked — declaring a function IMMUTABLE when it reads a table produces wrong results from cached plans, which is one of the nastier self-inflicted bugs in PostgreSQL. When in doubt use STABLE (same result within one statement) or the default VOLATILE.

STRICT handles NULL the way SQL expects: any NULL argument short-circuits to NULL without entering the body. Besides being correct, it is faster and it saves writing NULL guards inside every function.

The payoff is single-definition semantics. The discount rule now lives in the database, so a report, an application query, and a nightly job all compute it identically, and changing it from 10 to 12 percent is one CREATE OR REPLACE rather than a search across three codebases.

exercises.title: Add Branching and Local Variables with PL/pgSQL
exercises.problemHtml:
Plain SQL functions hold one expression. The moment a rule needs a local variable, a branch, a loop, or error handling, you switch to PL/pgSQL: a procedural language whose blocks are DECLARE / BEGIN / END and which can run any SQL between them.

Write stock_status(p_id INT) RETURNS TEXT that classifies a product by how much stock it has:

  • Declare a local variable for the stock level and load it with SELECT ... INTO.
  • If no product has that id, return unknown — test with NOT FOUND straight after the select.
  • Return out when stock is 0, low when it is 1 or 2, ok when it is 3 to 9, and plenty from 10 upwards, using IF / ELSIF / ELSE.
  • Call it for every product ordered by name, and once for the non-existent id 99.

Quote the body with $$ so the single quotes inside it need no escaping.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table: Cable stock 0, Keyboard 10, Monitor 2, Mouse 5.
exercises.outputSpec: Cable out, Keyboard plenty, Monitor low, Mouse ok — and unknown for id 99.
exercises.constraints: LANGUAGE plpgsql with a DECLARE section. Detect the missing row with NOT FOUND rather than counting first. Do not implement the branching with a CASE expression.

examplesJson.explanation: The zero branch is checked first, so an empty product never reads as low.
Two units falls in the 1–2 band.
SELECT INTO leaves the variable NULL and sets NOT FOUND, which the function turns into a domain answer instead of a NULL.

hintsJson: DECLARE names and types come before BEGIN; assignment uses := or SELECT ... INTO.
NOT FOUND is a boolean set by the previous SQL statement — check it immediately.
ELSIF is one word in PL/pgSQL, not ELSE IF.
Dollar quoting ($$ ... $$) means the body can contain quotes untouched.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
PL/pgSQL is the escape hatch from single-expression functions, and its shape is always the same: an optional DECLARE for local variables, a BEGIN … END block of statements, and dollar quoting around the whole body so the quotes inside need no doubling.

SELECT … INTO is the bridge between SQL and the procedural world, and its behaviour on a miss is the detail worth memorising: it does not raise an error, it leaves the variable NULL and sets the special boolean NOT FOUND. Testing that immediately is what turns "no such product" into a real answer instead of a NULL that propagates silently through the caller. If you would rather fail loudly, SELECT … INTO STRICT raises no_data_found instead — and also raises when more than one row matches, which is a useful guard.

The branch order matters as much as the conditions. Because ELSIF chains stop at the first true test, checking = 0 before <= 2 keeps an empty product from reporting low; swap them and the zero case becomes unreachable. That is the classic overlapping-range bug, and it is why the bands are written from the most specific outwards.

One note on cost: a plpgsql function is a black box to the planner — it cannot be inlined, and calling it once per row in a large query is measurably slower than an equivalent CASE expression. Reach for procedural code when you need branching over multiple statements, variables, exception handling or loops; keep single-expression rules in LANGUAGE sql where the planner can fold them away.

exercises.title: Return a Result Set with RETURNS TABLE
exercises.problemHtml:
A view cannot take arguments; a function can. RETURNS TABLE(...) declares a row shape, and RETURN QUERY streams the rows of a query into it, giving you a parameterised view you can join, filter and order like any table.

Write products_below(p_max NUMERIC) RETURNS TABLE(name TEXT, price NUMERIC, status TEXT):

  • Return every product whose price is at or below p_max, ordered by price descending, with the stock_status from the previous exercise as the third column.
  • Declare it STABLE: it reads tables, so it is not IMMUTABLE, but it returns the same rows throughout one statement.
  • Guard the argument: if p_max is negative, RETURN without rows rather than letting the query run.
  • Call it with 50 in a FROM clause, then filter its output further with a WHERE status <> 'out' to show it composes like a table.
  • Call it with -1 and report the row count.

Name the output columns so callers can reference them.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table: Cable 5.00 (stock 0), Mouse 20.00 (5), Keyboard 50.00 (10), Monitor 150.00 (2). The stock_status function from exercise 2 exists.
exercises.outputSpec: Called with 50: Keyboard 50.00 plenty, Mouse 20.00 ok, Cable 5.00 out. Filtered to exclude "out": Keyboard and Mouse. Called with -1: zero rows.
exercises.constraints: Use RETURNS TABLE with RETURN QUERY — do not return a refcursor or a text blob. The negative-argument guard must return no rows rather than raising.

examplesJson.output: Keyboard 50.00 plenty / Mouse 20.00 ok / Cable 5.00 out
examplesJson.explanation: Price descending, and the boundary value 50 is included because the test is <=.
The function composes like a table, so the caller can filter its result further.
The guard returns early, so a nonsensical argument yields an empty set rather than an error.

hintsJson: RETURNS TABLE(col type, ...) declares the shape; the names become the output columns.
RETURN QUERY streams a SELECT into that shape and execution continues afterwards.
A bare RETURN ends the function with whatever rows have been queued so far — none, here.
Qualify column references when a parameter or output column shares a name with a table column.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
RETURNS TABLE is how a function becomes a table source. The declared column names are real output names, so callers can SELECT status FROM products_below(50), join the result, or wrap it in a CTE — the composability that a function returning a formatted string or a JSON blob throws away.

RETURN QUERY appends the rows of a query to the result and then continues executing; it is not a return statement. That is what allows several RETURN QUERY calls in sequence, or a loop that emits rows per iteration. The bare RETURN in the guard ends the function with whatever has been queued — nothing — which is why a negative argument produces an empty set rather than an error. Returning empty versus raising is a design choice: empty composes better inside a larger query, while raising is clearer when the caller has made a programming mistake.

STABLE is the correct volatility for a function that reads tables but changes nothing. It tells the planner the result cannot change within a single statement, which permits caching the call for repeated identical arguments; IMMUTABLE would be a lie that survives until someone updates a price and a cached plan keeps the old answer.

Two practical notes. Qualify your column references — with output columns named name and price, an unqualified WHERE price <= p_max is ambiguous between the output column and the table column, and PostgreSQL raises rather than guessing. And be aware of the optimiser boundary: unlike a view, a plpgsql set-returning function is opaque, so an outer WHERE is applied after the function has produced all its rows. On a large table a view or an inlinable LANGUAGE sql function will usually plan better.

exercises.title: Use a PROCEDURE to Control Transactions
exercises.problemHtml:
A function runs inside the caller's transaction and therefore cannot commit — the whole call is one atomic unit. A procedure, invoked with CALL rather than SELECT, may issue COMMIT and ROLLBACK in its body. That difference is the entire reason procedures exist, and it is what makes batch jobs possible: process a thousand rows, commit every hundred, and a failure late in the run does not discard the work already done.

Write restock_all(p_amount INT, INOUT p_touched INT DEFAULT 0):

  • Loop over the product ids in ascending order with FOR … IN SELECT.
  • For each, add p_amount to stock and count the row in p_touched.
  • COMMIT after every second product, so a failure halfway through keeps the earlier updates.
  • Call it with 5 and report p_touched, then show the resulting stock levels ordered by name.
  • Finally show that a function cannot do this: attempt COMMIT inside a function, catch the error, and report its SQLSTATE.

An INOUT parameter is how a procedure returns a value — procedures have no RETURNS clause.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table with stock Keyboard 10, Mouse 5, Monitor 2, Cable 0.
exercises.outputSpec: Four products touched; stock becomes Cable 5, Keyboard 15, Monitor 7, Mouse 10; and the COMMIT attempt inside a function fails with SQLSTATE 2D000.
exercises.constraints: Use CREATE PROCEDURE and CALL. The commit must happen inside the loop. Do not emulate the return value with a temp table.

examplesJson.explanation: One iteration per product; the INOUT parameter carries the count back to the caller.

examplesJson.input: SELECT name, stock FROM products ORDER BY name
examplesJson.explanation: Every product gained 5 units, and the intermediate commits made the earlier ones durable before the loop finished.
A function runs inside the caller’s transaction and has no authority to end it.

hintsJson: A procedure has no RETURNS clause — declare INOUT parameters for values you want back.
CALL, not SELECT, invokes a procedure.
FOR rec IN SELECT ... LOOP ... END LOOP iterates over a query result.
Commit inside the loop, not after it, or the batching gains nothing.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The dividing line between a function and a procedure is transaction ownership. A function is called from within a running statement, which is itself inside a transaction, so ending that transaction from the inside is meaningless — PostgreSQL rejects it with SQLSTATE 2D000, "invalid transaction termination". A procedure invoked by CALL at the top level owns its transaction context and may commit and start a new one as it goes.

That capability is what makes procedures the right tool for batch work. Updating a million rows in one transaction holds locks for the whole run, bloats the write-ahead log, and loses everything if the last row fails. Committing every N rows keeps each unit small, releases locks promptly, and turns a failure into "the job stopped at row 700,000" rather than "the job did nothing". The trade is that the operation is no longer atomic as a whole — a restart must be able to resume, which is why real batch jobs record progress rather than assuming a clean run.

Procedures return values through INOUT parameters because they have no RETURNS clause. It reads oddly at first; the mental model is that CALL hands the parameter in and gets the modified value back, which is why the call site passes an initial 0.

Two cautions. COMMIT inside a procedure is only legal when the procedure was called at the top level — nest it inside an explicit BEGIN … COMMIT block from a client and the same error returns, because there is now an outer transaction it does not own. And a procedure that commits cannot be rolled back by the caller, so validation belongs before the first commit, not after it.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[client CALL restock_all] --> B[procedure owns the transaction]
  B --> C[update product 1]
  C --> D[update product 2 then COMMIT]
  D --> E[update product 3]
  E --> F[update product 4 then COMMIT]
  G[function called inside a statement] --> H[COMMIT rejected SQLSTATE 2D000]

exercises.title: Handle Errors with EXCEPTION Blocks and RAISE
exercises.problemHtml:
An unhandled error aborts the whole transaction. Inside PL/pgSQL a BEGIN … EXCEPTION WHEN … END block catches it instead, rolls back just that block, and lets the function decide what to do — turning a database error into a domain answer.

Write add_customer(p_id INT, p_name TEXT, p_email TEXT) RETURNS TEXT:

  • Insert the customer and return created.
  • Catch unique_violation and return duplicate instead of failing.
  • Catch any other error and return error: followed by the SQLSTATE.
  • Validate first: if p_email does not contain @, RAISE EXCEPTION with the message invalid email and the custom code 22000 — and catch it in the same block so the caller sees error:22000.

Then call it four times: a new customer, the same id again, the same email under a different id, and a bad email. Finally show the transaction survived by counting the customers.
exercises.inputSpec: The customers table holding Ann, Bob and Cy, with a unique constraint on email and the primary key on id.
exercises.outputSpec: created for the new customer, duplicate for the repeated id, duplicate for the repeated email, error:22000 for the malformed address, and a final count of 4 — the transaction was never aborted.
exercises.constraints: Catch by condition name, not by parsing the message text. The function must not let any error escape to the caller.

examplesJson.explanation: The insert succeeds and the function reports the outcome as data.
The primary key raises unique_violation, which the block catches by name.
The validation raises with a custom SQLSTATE and the generic handler reports it.

hintsJson: WHEN unique_violation catches SQLSTATE 23505 by its condition name.
WHEN OTHERS must come last — it matches everything except query cancellation.
SQLSTATE and SQLERRM are available inside the handler.
RAISE EXCEPTION ... USING ERRCODE = '22000' sets the code your handler will see.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Catching by condition name is the discipline that makes this robust. WHEN unique_violation is SQLSTATE 23505 under a readable alias; matching on the message text instead would break the first time the server is upgraded, localised, or the constraint is renamed. WHEN OTHERS belongs last and catches everything else — note that it deliberately does not catch query cancellation, so a statement timeout still works.

RAISE EXCEPTION … USING ERRCODE lets your own validation participate in the same mechanism. Assigning it a code means the handler treats a domain rule exactly like a database rule, and a caller in any language can branch on the SQLSTATE without string matching. Codes in the P0001 family, or the reserved user classes, are the conventional choice; 22000 is used here because it is a standard "data exception".

The final count is the point of the exercise. Each failed call rolled back only the failed insert; the transaction as a whole survived and the successful insert stands. That is what an exception block buys — without it, the first unique violation would abort the transaction and every later statement would fail with "current transaction is aborted".

The cost is worth knowing before you scatter these everywhere: a block with an EXCEPTION clause establishes an implicit subtransaction, which is not free. Inside a loop over a million rows the subtransaction overhead is measurable, and very large numbers of them can pressure the transaction id machinery. Prefer prevention where you can — INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING handles the duplicate case with no exception at all — and reserve handlers for outcomes you genuinely cannot test for in advance.

exercises.title: Record an Audit Trail with an AFTER Trigger
exercises.problemHtml:
A trigger is a function the database runs automatically when a table changes. Unlike application-side logging it cannot be bypassed — a manual psql session, a migration script, or a forgotten code path all fire it — which is what makes triggers the honest place for an audit trail.

Build one for products:

  • Write log_product_change() RETURNS TRIGGER that inserts one row into audit_log with the operation in lowercase (TG_OP gives INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), the entity product, and the affected id — from NEW for inserts and updates, from OLD for deletes.
  • Return NULL: the return value of an AFTER trigger is ignored.
  • Attach it with CREATE TRIGGER … AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON products FOR EACH ROW.
  • Exercise it: insert a product, update its price, delete it.
  • Show the trail — action and entity_id ordered by id — and the total row count.

Drop the trigger at the end so later exercises are unaffected.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table with four rows and an empty audit_log.
exercises.outputSpec: Three audit rows in order — insert 5, update 5, delete 5 — and a count of 3.
exercises.constraints: One trigger covering all three operations. Read the id from NEW or OLD as appropriate; referencing NEW in a delete raises an error.

examplesJson.explanation: The AFTER trigger fires once per row and records the new id from NEW.

examplesJson.input: UPDATE products SET price = 70 WHERE id = 5
examplesJson.explanation: TG_OP distinguishes the operation inside a single trigger function.
NEW is not defined for a delete, so the id must come from OLD.

hintsJson: A trigger function takes no arguments and returns the pseudo-type TRIGGER.
TG_OP holds the operation name; lower() it for a tidy log.
NEW exists for INSERT and UPDATE, OLD for UPDATE and DELETE.
An AFTER trigger’s return value is ignored, so return NULL.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The strength of a trigger is that it is not optional. Application-level auditing records what the application did; a trigger records what happened, including the emergency update someone ran by hand at 2am. For an audit trail that a regulator or an incident review will rely on, that difference is the whole point.

Three trigger-specific variables do the work. TG_OP names the operation, which is what lets one function serve all three events instead of three near-identical ones. NEW is the post-change row and OLD the pre-change row: an insert has only NEW, a delete only OLD, and an update both. Referencing the wrong one is the standard beginner error and it fails at run time, not at creation time, so the delete path is worth testing deliberately.

AFTER versus BEFORE matters here. An AFTER trigger runs once the row change is applied and constraints have been checked, so it never records a change that was subsequently rejected — exactly what an audit log needs. Its return value is ignored, hence RETURN NULL. A BEFORE trigger, covered next, runs earlier and can alter or veto the row, which makes it the wrong choice for logging.

Two limits to keep in mind. FOR EACH ROW means a thousand-row update fires the function a thousand times, so keep the body cheap; a FOR EACH STATEMENT trigger with transition tables (REFERENCING NEW TABLE AS …) is the set-based alternative when volume matters. And triggers are invisible in the calling code, which is their power and their hazard: a slow or failing trigger turns a simple UPDATE into a mystery, so they deserve the same documentation and testing as the tables they guard.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[INSERT UPDATE or DELETE on products] --> B[row change applied]
  B --> C[constraints checked]
  C --> D[AFTER trigger fires per row]
  D --> E{TG_OP}
  E -->|DELETE| F[read OLD id]
  E -->|INSERT or UPDATE| G[read NEW id]
  F --> H[insert audit row]
  G --> H

exercises.title: Normalise Data with a BEFORE Trigger
concepts: vetoing a row by returning NULL
exercises.problemHtml:
A BEFORE trigger runs before the row is written and can change it: whatever it returns is what gets stored. Returning NULL silently skips the row entirely. That makes it the tool for invariants that must hold no matter which client wrote the data — trimming, case-folding, defaulting, or rejecting.

Guard the customers table:

  • Write tidy_customer() RETURNS TRIGGER that trims whitespace from NEW.name, lowercases and trims NEW.email, and returns NEW.
  • If the trimmed name is empty, skip the row by returning NULL — the insert is silently discarded.
  • Attach it BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE … FOR EACH ROW.
  • Insert ' Dee ' with email ' DEE@X.IO ' and show what was actually stored.
  • Insert a row whose name is only spaces and show the row count did not change.
  • Update Ann's email to 'ANN@NEW.IO' and show it was folded too.

Drop the trigger afterwards.
exercises.inputSpec: The customers table after exercise 5 added Di — four rows: Ann, Bob, Cy, Di — before the trigger is attached.
exercises.outputSpec: Dee is stored as "Dee" with email "dee@x.io"; the blank-name insert changes nothing so the table still holds five rows; and Ann’s updated email is stored lowercased.
exercises.constraints: Normalisation must happen in the trigger, not in the INSERT statements. Return NEW to store the modified row, NULL to skip it.

examplesJson.input: INSERT INTO customers VALUES (7, '  Dee  ', '  DEE@X.IO ')
examplesJson.explanation: The trigger rewrites NEW before the row reaches the table.
Returning NULL from a BEFORE trigger discards the row without raising an error.

examplesJson.input: UPDATE customers SET email = 'ANN@NEW.IO' WHERE id = 1
examplesJson.explanation: The same trigger covers updates, so the invariant holds for every write path.

hintsJson: Assign to NEW.column inside the function; the assignment is what changes the stored row.
Return NEW at the end or nothing is written.
Returning NULL skips the row silently — use RAISE instead when the caller should know.
One trigger on INSERT OR UPDATE keeps the rule in a single place.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The defining property of a BEFORE … FOR EACH ROW trigger is that its return value becomes the row. Assigning to NEW.email and returning NEW stores the rewritten value; forgetting the RETURN NEW returns NULL instead, which silently discards every row — a spectacular and confusing bug, because nothing errors and the table simply stays empty.

That silent skip is also a feature when used deliberately, as with the blank name here, but it deserves care: the caller is told the insert succeeded. When the client should know the row was rejected, RAISE EXCEPTION is the honest choice and a CHECK constraint is better still, because constraints are declarative, visible in the schema, and enforced without executing procedural code.

The reason to normalise at this layer rather than in the application is coverage. Case-folding emails in one service does nothing about the admin tool, the CSV import, or the migration script — and the day two rows differ only by case, the unique constraint that was supposed to prevent duplicates has already failed to. A BEFORE trigger applies to every write path by construction.

Two operational notes. Covering INSERT OR UPDATE in one trigger keeps the rule in a single place; an insert-only trigger leaves a hole an update walks straight through. And where the transformation is a pure function of the row, PostgreSQL offers a declarative alternative worth preferring: a generated column, or a unique index on lower(email), both of which the planner understands and neither of which can be forgotten.

exercises.title: Build Dynamic SQL Safely with EXECUTE and format
exercises.problemHtml:
Some procedures must decide at run time which table or column to touch — a maintenance routine over many tables, a generic counter, an archiving job. That requires building SQL as text and running it with EXECUTE, and it is exactly where injection enters a database that was otherwise safe.

The rules are mechanical: %I in format() quotes an identifier, %L quotes a literal, and values are better passed out-of-band with USING, which parameterises them properly.

Write two functions and compare them:

  • row_count(p_table TEXT) RETURNS BIGINT — builds SELECT count(*) FROM %I with format and runs it with EXECUTE … INTO. Call it for products and customers.
  • find_by(p_table TEXT, p_column TEXT, p_value TEXT) RETURNS BIGINT — quotes the table and column with %I and passes the value through USING, returning the number of matching rows. Call it for customers, name, Ann.
  • Attack it: call find_by with the value ' OR 1=1 -- and show the count is 0, not the whole table.
  • Attack the identifier too: call row_count with products; DROP TABLE customers; --, catch the error, and report the SQLSTATE. Then show customers is still there.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table (4 rows) and the customers table (5 rows: Ann, Bob, Cy, Di from exercise 5 and Dee from exercise 7).
exercises.outputSpec: row_count returns 4 for products and 5 for customers; find_by finds 1 Ann; the injected value matches 0 rows; and the injected table name fails with SQLSTATE 42P01 while customers survives.
exercises.constraints: Never concatenate the arguments into the SQL string. Identifiers go through %I, values through USING.

examplesJson.explanation: The identifier is quoted and the value is a bound parameter.
The hostile string is compared as a literal name; it matches nothing and changes no SQL.
%I quotes the whole thing as one identifier, so it becomes a table name that does not exist rather than two statements.

hintsJson: format('SELECT count(*) FROM %I', p_table) quotes the identifier correctly, including odd characters.
EXECUTE ... INTO stores a single-row result into a variable.
Pass values with USING $1 rather than embedding them, even quoted.
A quoted identifier cannot contain a statement separator — that is what stops the DROP TABLE.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Dynamic SQL is unavoidable when the structure of a statement depends on run-time input, because a bound parameter can stand for a value but never for a table or column name. That is precisely the gap injection walks through, and format() is the tool that closes it.

The two placeholders do different jobs. %I renders its argument as a single quoted identifier: products; DROP TABLE customers; -- becomes "products; DROP TABLE customers; --", one table name containing punctuation, which simply does not exist — hence SQLSTATE 42P01, undefined_table, and a customers table that is still there. %L renders a properly quoted literal. Naive concatenation with || offers neither, and is the one construct to treat as forbidden here.

For values, USING is better than %L: the value travels as a bound parameter, so it is never part of the SQL text, the plan can be reused, and no quoting question arises. The injected ' OR 1=1 -- is therefore compared as a customer name, matches nobody, and returns 0 — the same demonstration as in an application language, one layer lower.

Two additional cautions for real use. Quoting an identifier does not authorise it: row_count('secret_salaries') is well-formed and will happily count a table the caller should never see, so validate the argument against an allowlist or against information_schema when the input is untrusted. And SECURITY DEFINER functions — which run with the owner's privileges — must additionally pin search_path, or a caller can create a same-named table in a schema earlier on the path and have your dynamic SQL find theirs instead.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[run time table and column names] --> B[format with %I quotes identifiers]
  C[user supplied value] --> D[USING binds it as a parameter]
  B --> E[EXECUTE builds one valid statement]
  D --> E
  E --> F[injection attempt becomes a missing table or a non matching value]

exercises.title: Replace a Row-by-Row Loop with One Set-Based Statement
exercises.problemHtml:
Procedural languages invite procedural thinking: fetch the rows, loop, update each one. It works, and on anything larger than a demo it is dramatically slower than the single statement that expresses the same intent — every iteration is a separate plan, execute and round trip inside the engine.

Implement the same price adjustment twice and compare:

  • markup_loop(p_pct NUMERIC) RETURNS INT — loop over the products with FOR rec IN SELECT id, price …, computing the new price in PL/pgSQL and issuing one UPDATE per row. Return the number of rows touched.
  • markup_set(p_pct NUMERIC) RETURNS INT — one UPDATE products SET price = ROUND(price * (1 + p_pct/100), 2), returning the affected count with GET DIAGNOSTICS … ROW_COUNT.
  • Apply markup_loop(10) and show the resulting prices ordered by name.
  • Apply markup_set(-10) to undo it approximately, show the prices again, and report both functions' return values.
  • Print same_rows — whether the two functions reported the same count.

Both must produce the same row count; the point is that only one of them scales.
exercises.inputSpec: The products table after the restock exercise: Cable 5.00, Keyboard 50.00, Monitor 150.00, Mouse 20.00 — four rows.
exercises.outputSpec: After the loop markup of 10 percent: Cable 5.50, Keyboard 55.00, Monitor 165.00, Mouse 22.00. After the set-based -10 percent: Cable 4.95, Keyboard 49.50, Monitor 148.50, Mouse 19.80. Both functions report 4 rows and same_rows is true.
exercises.constraints: The loop version must genuinely issue one UPDATE per row. The set-based version must be a single statement and must read its count from GET DIAGNOSTICS.

examplesJson.explanation: Four iterations, four UPDATE statements, four rows touched.
One statement touches the same four rows; GET DIAGNOSTICS reports how many.
A 10 percent rise followed by a 10 percent cut does not restore the original — percentages do not compose that way, which is a data bug the loop hides just as well as the set version.

hintsJson: FOR rec IN SELECT ... LOOP gives you rec.id and rec.price per iteration.
GET DIAGNOSTICS n = ROW_COUNT reads the count of the statement just executed.
Round in both versions or the results will differ in the last decimal.
Compare the two counts programmatically rather than by eye.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Both functions produce identical results, and that is what makes the comparison instructive: correctness does not distinguish them, cost does. The loop pays per-row overhead — planning and executing a separate UPDATE, re-locating the row by primary key, firing any triggers individually — while the set-based statement plans once and lets the executor sweep the table. At four rows the difference is invisible; at four million the loop takes minutes and the single statement takes seconds. Worse, the loop's cost is linear in a way that hides in testing, because test data is always small.

The habit worth building is to ask what the loop is for. If the body is "compute a value from this row and write it back", the computation belongs in the UPDATE itself — with UPDATE … FROM when it needs other tables, or a CTE when it needs several steps. Genuine reasons to loop are rarer than they look: calling an external function per row, committing in batches (exercise 4), or an algorithm whose step N depends on the result of step N−1.

GET DIAGNOSTICS … ROW_COUNT is how the set-based version reports what it did. It reads the row count of the statement immediately preceding it, so it must come first — any intervening statement resets it. That single line is often the only bookkeeping a batch job needs, and it removes the usual excuse for looping "so I can count them".

The output also carries a data lesson worth noticing: raising by 10 percent and then cutting by 10 percent leaves Keyboard at 49.50, not 50.00, because the second percentage applies to the larger base. Reversible price adjustments need the original value stored, not an inverse percentage — a bug both implementations share, which is a reminder that rewriting a loop for speed does not make wrong logic right.

exercises.title: Capstone: An Order-Placement Procedure That Cannot Oversell
exercises.problemHtml:
Bring the module together: one entry point that validates its inputs, writes several tables consistently, refuses to break an invariant, and records what happened.

Write place_order(p_customer INT, p_product INT, p_qty INT, INOUT p_result TEXT DEFAULT ''):

  • Reject a non-positive quantity by raising with ERRCODE '22023', caught in the same procedure so p_result becomes error:22023.
  • Return no-customer or no-product when either id is missing — use SELECT … INTO and NOT FOUND.
  • Reserve stock with a conditional update: UPDATE products SET stock = stock - p_qty WHERE id = p_product AND stock >= p_qty. If GET DIAGNOSTICS reports zero rows, the stock was insufficient — set p_result to insufficient-stock and stop, without ever writing a negative value.
  • Otherwise insert the order and its item, set total to price × quantity, write an audit_log row with action order, and set p_result to ok: followed by the order id.
  • Wrap the writes so any unexpected error rolls them back and reports error: plus the SQLSTATE.

Then call it four times: a valid order for 2 keyboards, an order for 99 monitors, an order with quantity 0, and an order for a missing product 42. Report each result, then the remaining stock, the number of orders, and the audit count.
exercises.inputSpec: Products after the markup exercise — Keyboard 49.50 stock 15, Monitor 148.50 stock 7, Mouse 19.80 stock 10, Cable 4.95 stock 5 — customers Ann, Bob, Cy, Di and Dee, and an audit_log holding the three rows from exercise 6.
exercises.outputSpec: The valid order returns ok:1 and leaves Keyboard with 13 in stock; the 99-monitor order returns insufficient-stock and leaves Monitor untouched at 7; quantity 0 returns error:22023; the unknown product returns no-product. One order row exists and the audit log has gained exactly one row.
exercises.constraints: The stock guard must be the WHERE clause of the update, not a separate SELECT followed by an UPDATE. Every failure path must leave the tables unchanged.

examplesJson.explanation: The conditional update succeeds, the order and item are written, and the audit row records it.
The update matches no row because the WHERE clause fails, so nothing is written and no negative stock is possible.
The existence check returns a domain answer rather than letting a foreign key error escape.

hintsJson: Put the stock condition in the WHERE clause so the check and the write are one atomic statement.
GET DIAGNOSTICS immediately after the UPDATE tells you whether the guard passed.
RETURNING ... INTO gives you the generated order id without a second query.
Catch your own RAISE in the same block so the caller always gets a result string.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql
starterCodeJson.code:
-- Write the function, procedure or trigger described above.
CREATE OR REPLACE /* FUNCTION or PROCEDURE */ ...
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  -- TODO
END;
$$;

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The heart of this procedure is one line: the stock condition lives in the WHERE clause of the update rather than in a preceding SELECT. The read-then-write version — check that stock is 99 or more, then subtract — is correct in a single-user demo and wrong under concurrency, because two sessions can both read 100 and both subtract 99. Folding the check into the update makes it a single atomic statement: the row is locked as it is matched, so the second caller's WHERE sees the already-decremented value, matches nothing, and reports zero rows. GET DIAGNOSTICS then turns "no rows matched" into the domain answer insufficient-stock. This is the same idea as a compare-and-swap, expressed in SQL, and it is why no CHECK (stock >= 0) violation can ever occur here.

The rest is layering. Validation raises with a custom SQLSTATE and is caught by the procedure's own handler, so the caller receives a result string in every case rather than sometimes a value and sometimes an exception — a boundary that is far easier to consume from application code. Existence checks use NOT FOUND to return domain answers instead of letting a foreign-key violation escape as a raw database error. And the three writes — order, item, audit row — succeed or fail together, because an unhandled error inside the block rolls the block back.

Note what the exception handler costs and covers. It establishes a subtransaction, so the failed work is undone while the caller's session stays usable; without it, one bad call would poison the whole transaction. But WHEN OTHERS is a blunt instrument: it also swallows programming errors such as a typo'd column, reporting them as error:42703 rather than crashing loudly. In production, catch the conditions you expect by name and re-raise the rest.

Finally, the audit row is written inside the same unit as the change, so the trail cannot record an order that was rolled back — the same guarantee the trigger in exercise 6 gives, obtained here by construction. Whether to use a trigger or explicit writes is a real design choice: triggers cannot be bypassed but are invisible at the call site, while explicit writes are obvious in the code but easy to omit in the next procedure someone adds.
